title: RNC Ballot Strategy and the Push for Election Integrity
content:
Election officials emphasize the need for flexible federal funding to ensure smooth elections. Kentucky Secretary of State Michael Adams noted that running federal, state, and local elections simultaneously requires modernization. This modernization is best supported when Congress provides unconditional funding. During the COVID-19 pandemic, funding from HAVA grants and the CARES Act played a critical role in helping Kentucky adapt to unprecedented challenges. For example, Adams implemented excuse-free absentee voting and three weeks of open polls, resulting in the highest primary turnout since 2008.

He later worked with Democratic Governor Andy Beshear to make some of these changes permanent, despite opposition from within his own party. For his efforts, Adams was awarded the John F. Kennedy Profile in Courage in 2024. He emphasized that voting should be as nonpartisan as renewing a driver's license. Meanwhile, the Republican National Committee (RNC) faces a different challenge: restoring trust among voters who remain highly skeptical of the election system. The RNC has invested millions of dollars in a litigation machine that aims to change voting rules under the banner of election integrity.

However, critics argue these legal efforts are designed to make it harder to vote and easier to undermine election results. If the Supreme Court adopts the RNC's legal theories, hundreds of thousands of eligible Americans could be disenfranchised due to postal delays beyond their control. Despite these legal pushes, it remains uncertain whether such rulings will effectively restore voter faith in elections. Republicans see these efforts as necessary to make the system more acceptable to their base. Conversely, some experts warn that Supreme Court rulings might make voting more confusing, further suppressing turnout.

Skeptics point out that many of these legal battles focus on issues with relatively small impacts. Ultimately, only time will tell if the RNC's substantial investments in election integrity litigation will influence voter trust or inadvertently complicate the voting process.
